method_atom: A deterministic redistribution process on a discrete lattice in which a locally conserved quantity accumulated at sites is repeatedly balanced by fixed local rules (toppling / passing to neighbours) until every site satisfies a stability threshold, yielding a final occupied region. The transferable machinery is proving that this region converges to an isotropic (spherical) limit shape by separately bounding an inscribed inner radius from below and a circumscribed outer radius from above with sharp error terms, giving near-optimal control of the boundary fluctuations.
object_atom: Discrete growth and aggregation models on the integer lattice: the rotor-router aggregation model (a deterministic analogue of random walk and of internal diffusion-limited aggregation), the divisible sandpile, and the classical abelian sandpile, in which n particles or units of mass occupy lattice sites.
brief_reason: The deterministic local-balancing dynamics plus inner/outer-radius shape-convergence proof technique carries across fields, while the specific rotor-router and sandpile lattice-aggregation models are the discrete-probability/statistical-mechanics objects it is applied to.
